comments:
Summary and strengths:
This paper studies the effectiveness of very small feed-forward neural networks for a range of NLP tasks (language identification, POS tagging, Chinese word segmentation, and preordering for SMT). The authors present a compact architecture based on hashed character n-gram embeddings, a single hidden layer, and task-specific transition systems for structured tasks. They evaluate model size, FLOPs, and wallclock throughput, and introduce practical techniques to reduce memory (quantization of embeddings, Bloom-mapped word clusters) and improve accuracy under tight budgets. Results show that these tiny models can approach state-of-the-art or strong baselines while offering large speed and memory improvements, and the paper provides useful ablation/analysis across tasks.

Weaknesses and concerns:
- Originality is modest: the work combines several known practical techniques (character n-grams, hashing, quantization, Bloom filters, shallow feed-forward nets) and applies them systematically. The novel contributions are mainly engineering choices and two task-specific transition systems rather than fundamentally new learning algorithms.
- Comparisons are reasonable but could be strengthened. For some tasks (e.g., preordering), the impact on downstream translation quality is not shown, and comparisons to other compact sequence models beyond the BTS LSTM baseline are limited.
- While FLOPs and CPU throughput are reported, more discussion of real-device performance (mobile CPU, memory bandwidth, energy) would strengthen claims about resource-constrained environments. The impact of the quantization/dequantization step on latency in such environments is asserted but not thoroughly measured.
- Some methodological details (training stability, variance across runs, statistical significance) are not emphasized; additional error analysis or failure modes would be useful for practitioners.

Suggestions for improvement:
- Include an evaluation of end-to-end impact for the preordering task on downstream MT quality (BLEU) to demonstrate practical benefit.
- Add experiments or discussion on runtime/memory/energy on representative mobile hardware (or at least mobile-like constraints) to substantiate deployment claims.
- Provide more analysis of error types, per-language breakdowns where relevant, and variance across random seeds.
- Clarify and highlight the new transition systems (pseudo-code or diagrams) in the main paper to improve reproducibility.

Overall assessment:
The paper provides a solid, practically-oriented contribution: a careful, well-documented study showing that very small feed-forward models are a strong and efficient baseline for many NLP tasks. It will be useful to researchers and practitioners working under tight memory/latency constraints. Given its empirical thoroughness and practical value, I recommend acceptance.
